genre_assessment: Primary genre: queer campus sports romance. Secondary stack: enemies-to-lovers, trauma-healing love story, scholarship pressure, abusive-family escape, and found-family redemption.
tone: Cold, witty, bruised, intimate, anxious, and gradually thawing into hard-won tenderness.
worldbuilding_seeds: New England college campuses connected by hockey culture, dorm intimacy, and winter running routines
A masculinity-heavy sports world where image, toughness, and rumor control social survival
Religious-family pressure and small-town surveillance operating against queer self-definition
Found family existing in contrast to biological family violence
timeline_seeds: High school: Landon targets Wren's brother under pressure from his father and internalized homophobia
Pre-college: Landon comes out, nearly gets sent to conversion therapy, and becomes emotionally shut down
Freshman fall: Wren and Landon begin colliding on runs, at coffee shops, and across rival campuses
Mid-season: attraction, jealousy, and emotional dependence grow alongside hockey pressure
Late season: love confession misfires, breakup lands, and Landon's family violence detonates the endgame
Epilogue: Landon plays hockey on his own terms and remains openly with Wren

visual_theme_seeds: fluorescent masculinity versus soft private light
books and hockey gear in the same emotional frame

character_cards.name: Wren Ridley
character_cards.public_identity: Sharp, observant Brown student and aspiring writer with a dry mouth
character_cards.hidden_identity: Emotionally avoidant loner who is much softer than he pretends
character_cards.core_desire: To be chosen honestly without surrendering control
character_cards.leverage: social intelligence, language, calmness under pressure, family support

character_cards.name: Landon Reilly
character_cards.public_identity: Talented but cold Providence hockey player with anger issues
character_cards.hidden_identity: Traumatized queer son still living inside his father's voice
character_cards.core_desire: To be safe, wanted, and free to want what he wants
character_cards.leverage: physicality, raw sincerity, athletic value, willingness to protect his sister

character_cards.name: James
character_cards.public_identity: Wren's unserious roommate and former hookup
character_cards.hidden_identity: Unexpectedly perceptive witness to Wren's emotional unraveling
character_cards.leverage: proximity to Wren and social opportunism

character_cards.name: Stella
character_cards.hidden_identity: A stability anchor who sees more than Wren likes
character_cards.core_desire: To keep Wren from sabotaging himself

character_cards.name: Micah Hanson
character_cards.public_identity: Landon's longtime friend with a healthy family around him
character_cards.hidden_identity: The person who knows exactly how damaged Landon is
character_cards.core_desire: To keep Landon alive, stable, and connected
character_cards.leverage: trust, access, and his family home

character_cards.name: Olivia Reilly
character_cards.public_identity: Landon's younger sister still trapped at home
character_cards.hidden_identity: A second victim whose danger escalates the climax
character_cards.core_desire: To escape the family house intact
character_cards.leverage: knowledge of the father's abuse and ability to force action

character_cards.name: Peter Reilly
character_cards.public_identity: Strict Christian father obsessed with discipline and image
character_cards.hidden_identity: Abuser who weaponizes religion, hockey ambition, and control
character_cards.core_desire: To force Landon back into the version of son he can own
character_cards.leverage: family terror, shame conditioning, and physical violence

relationship_map.dynamic: enemy memory becomes flirtation, then care, then love complicated by fear
suspicion and fascination turn into dependence and eventual emotional exposure
casual sexual history that later becomes jealousy fuel
protective friendship with zero patience for self-deception
older-brother protection shaped by shared abuse
authoritarian abuse, shame, and ownership masquerading as parenting
control and intimidation that later force the escape plot

conflict_map.label: old violence debt
conflict_map.description: Wren knows Landon once targeted his brother in a homophobic incident, making the romance morally charged from the start.

conflict_map.label: self-hatred versus desire
conflict_map.description: Landon wants Wren while hearing his father's disgust in his head every time intimacy becomes real.

conflict_map.label: casual versus serious love mismatch
conflict_map.description: Wren can perform cool intelligence and sexual confidence, but freezes when emotional permanence is demanded.

conflict_map.label: sports-world exposure risk
conflict_map.description: Hockey culture, gossip, and teammates weaponize masculinity and sexuality as pressure tools.

conflict_map.label: family abuse emergency
conflict_map.description: The father shifts from psychological control to physical violence, forcing the romance into rescue mode.

selling_points: icy enemies-to-lovers dynamic with real emotional bite
college hockey masculinity contrasted with private softness
family abuse survival thread that raises the third-act stakes
found family warmth as payoff against biological-family cruelty
queer romance centered on thaw, not tragedy
similarity_risks: Direct reuse of the morning run collision as the adaptation opening
Direct reuse of drunken sleepover and hangover intimacy beats
Direct reuse of the father beating Landon and Wren immediately rescuing him in the same shape
Direct reuse of the delayed 'I love you' confession plus lost letter beat
Direct reuse of Brown versus Providence college geography and hockey structure
source_value_assessment: High adaptation value. The source has a strong emotional engine, a clean central couple, a built-in external antagonist, and a premium cold-to-warm visual language. The weakness is that the original progression is novelistic and routine-based, so it needs a more aggressively public, cliffhanger-driven plot engine for short-drama conversion.
supporting_evidence: Chapter 1 establishes the hostile run-in, Wren's defensive wit, and the brother-history obstacle.
Chapter 2 anchors Landon's repression, Micah as safe contact, and the abusive-family context.
Chapters 10-23 prove the chemistry engine works through banter, proximity, jealousy, and softening.
Chapter 49 supplies the love-confession fracture and shows Wren's emotional blockage clearly.
Chapters 51-53 escalate the father into a fully externalized antagonist and turn Olivia into a rescue trigger.
The epilogue confirms the emotionally satisfying direction: open relationship, healthier hockey life, and a thawed self-image.
